text.title: The 5 Nielsen heuristics chosen for per-feature audit
text.summary: Five of the ten Nielsen heuristics are concretely checkable per feature. The other five stay in education material because checking all 10 per feature is theatre.

list.item: N1 Visibility of system status — "the user always knows what the system is doing right now".
N3 User control & freedom — "the user can undo / cancel / exit without consequence".
N4 Consistency & standards — "the same word means the same thing across the feature and the rest of the app".
N5 Error prevention — "the design makes the bad input hard to enter, not just hard to submit".
N6 Recognition over recall — "the user does not have to remember information from one step to the next".

rule.statement: The Nielsen audit section in ui-ux-design.md MUST contain exactly these five rows. Adding rows for N2/N7/N8/N9/N10 is forbidden in this per-feature template.
rule.rationale: Bounded checklists get done; long checklists get faked. Five is the empirical sweet spot.

section.title: Why these five (not the other five)
section.p: N1, N3, N5, N6 catch the most common per-feature regressions (silent operations, no-cancel modals, validate-on-submit, wizards without breadcrumb context). N4 catches the cross-feature drift that explodes after enough small changes. The remaining five (match real world, error recovery, flexibility, aesthetic, help docs) are either site-wide concerns or rarely actionable per feature.

antipattern.reason: The author runs out of attention by N6 and the last four become "ok" without inspection. Five rows that are taken seriously beat ten rows that are not.
